Mostly because there is one constraint, until gcc upstream fixes the issue: we need to use old ABI for compatibility purposes, which excludes a few very specific bleeding edge features. I can find a gcc bugzilla ticket, I think upstream folks mentioned existing differences at the moment.
Having said that, in 99.9% of cases everything just works. Sometimes better than with Apple `libc++` of macOS 13 :) 

I can do the testing of any suggested fixes on my end. It is understandable that setting up the environment on your side is pretty involving and hardly justiciable.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2466106580/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Perhaps no need to further debug it on Linux, looks like it works fine there.

I vaguely recall macOS had some specific re complex and double types (from BLAS-related issues), I will look into that. Commit which I referenced above seems to have changed type handling (I may be wrong, did not review it thoroughly yet). Unfortunately, I cannot easily test the build on a modern macOS with `libstdc++`, since that gonna require building literally everything starting from gcc, and Apple made it non-trivial to avoid `libc++` now: system headers do not work well with `libstdc++`, or at least it was the case last time I tried to set up MacPorts on Sonoma arm64 against `libstdc++`.
What may in fact work is testing this on 10.6.8 Intel, where I kinda have `libstdc++` setup, and can use prebuilt MacPorts one with `libc++`. If neither has issues, then the only factor left is the SDK of my version of 10.6. ### Reproducing:

Build NumPy 1.20.3 on OpenBSD using its usual compiler, clang 10.0.1, and (e)gfortran.
The resulting NumPy's f2py is buggy. It fails the test in #18179, and also builds of SciPy using it are
broken, see https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14364

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2024-11-08T03:33:03Z","updated_at":"2024-11-10T07:53:09Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Downstream in SciPy (see: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21830), when using GNU toolchain version `14.2.0` from homebrew, I consistently see a SciPy build failure on MacOS ARM because of `scipy/linalg/_flapackmodule.c:90:10: fatal error: threads.h: No such file or directory`.

I made a temporary hack (below) to my local NumPy/`f2py` source that fixed the SciPy build issue. I'm not sure if that's exactly what we'd want to do, though [GNU docs on the matter](https://www.gnu.org/software/gnulib/manual/html_node/threads_002eh.html#:~:text=This%20header%20file%20is%20missing,%2C%20MSVC%2014%2C%20Android%209.0.) do suggest that `threads.h` is missing on MacOS 14. This part of `f2py` has caused pain a few times in the past per gh-19437 and gh-24761.

```diff
--- a/numpy/f2py/cfuncs.py
+++ b/numpy/f2py/cfuncs.py
@@ -558,7 +558,8 @@ def errmess(s: str) -> None:
       && (__STDC_VERSION__ >= 201112L) \\
       && !defined(__STDC_NO_THREADS__) \\
       && (!defined(__GLIBC__) || __GLIBC__ > 2 || (__GLIBC__ == 2 && __GLIBC_MINOR__ > 12)) \\
-      && !defined(NPY_OS_OPENBSD) && !defined(NPY_OS_HAIKU)
+      && !defined(NPY_OS_OPENBSD) && !defined(NPY_OS_HAIKU) \\
+      && !(defined(__APPLE__) && (__MAC_OS_X_VERSION_MAX_ALLOWED >= 140000 && __MAC_OS_X_VERSION_MAX_ALLOWED < 150000))
 /* __STDC_NO_THREADS__ was first defined in a maintenance release of glibc 2.12,
    see https://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/commit-hurd/2012-07/msg00180.html,
    so `!defined(__STDC_NO_THREADS__)` may give false positive for the existence
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2466628070","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27718#issuecomment-2466628070","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718","id":2466628070,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86TBcHm","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-10T07:53:08Z","updated_at":"2024-11-10T07:53:08Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"`threads.h` is also missing on earlier macOS, and the source erroneously assumes it is present due to C version.

> 
> Yes, this is correct indeed, and the bug is that `__STDC_NO_THREADS__` isn't set despite no `threads.h`. This is the compilers job I believe (e.g., MSVC takes care of defining it), so it's the GCC build in use that has the bug.

Discussion with gcc upstream: https://gcc.gnu.org/bugzilla/show_bug.cgi?id=53769

> I think this was probably always broken, but no one noticed because they weren't yet compiling with `-std=c11` or higher (plus using GCC on macOS is rare).

I think this is just untypical to use this header. GCC support emulated TLS on system where it is not supported otherwise.

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":13,"created_at":"2024-11-08T03:33:03Z","updated_at":"2024-11-10T12:14:13Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Downstream in SciPy (see: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21830), when using GNU toolchain version `14.2.0` from homebrew, I consistently see a SciPy build failure on MacOS ARM because of `scipy/linalg/_flapackmodule.c:90:10: fatal error: threads.h: No such file or directory`.

I made a temporary hack (below) to my local NumPy/`f2py` source that fixed the SciPy build issue. I'm not sure if that's exactly what we'd want to do, though [GNU docs on the matter](https://www.gnu.org/software/gnulib/manual/html_node/threads_002eh.html#:~:text=This%20header%20file%20is%20missing,%2C%20MSVC%2014%2C%20Android%209.0.) do suggest that `threads.h` is missing on MacOS 14. This part of `f2py` has caused pain a few times in the past per gh-19437 and gh-24761.

```diff
--- a/numpy/f2py/cfuncs.py
+++ b/numpy/f2py/cfuncs.py
@@ -558,7 +558,8 @@ def errmess(s: str) -> None:
       && (__STDC_VERSION__ >= 201112L) \\
       && !defined(__STDC_NO_THREADS__) \\
       && (!defined(__GLIBC__) || __GLIBC__ > 2 || (__GLIBC__ == 2 && __GLIBC_MINOR__ > 12)) \\
-      && !defined(NPY_OS_OPENBSD) && !defined(NPY_OS_HAIKU)
+      && !defined(NPY_OS_OPENBSD) && !defined(NPY_OS_HAIKU) \\
+      && !(defined(__APPLE__) && (__MAC_OS_X_VERSION_MAX_ALLOWED >= 140000 && __MAC_OS_X_VERSION_MAX_ALLOWED < 150000))
 /* __STDC_NO_THREADS__ was first defined in a maintenance release of glibc 2.12,
    see https://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/commit-hurd/2012-07/msg00180.html,
    so `!defined(__STDC_NO_THREADS__)` may give false positive for the existence
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2466711342","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27718#issuecomment-2466711342","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718","id":2466711342,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86TBwcu","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-10T12:14:12Z","updated_at":"2024-11-10T12:14:12Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> _Thread_local

I think gcc normally uses this one, yes.

I do not understand at the moment why that happens: could be a bug in `legacysupport` headers, could be some compatibility issue specifically here (after all, normally it works fine).

`legacysupport` was needed due to `scipy` using some functions missing in older macOS (`sincos` and `strnlen`). AFAICT, `sincos` is not used directly anymore, and for `strnlen` a trivial chunk could be added into the sources, but since those modules are generated, I do not know how to patch them.
However, I added required flags into `mesonbuild` files for specific modules which use `strnlen`, so that those use `legacysupport` implementation and link to the library, but it is not visible to the build overall.

So the build worked now.

Thank you again for testing, that helped to point into the right direction. Otherwise I would have still dug into C++ ABI issues, assuming it is caused by that.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2466715456/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27718","id":2642730510,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86dhN4O","number":27718,"title":"BUG: f2py threads.h on MacOS 14.x may need a shim?","user":{"login":"tylerjereddy","id":7903078,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc5MDMwNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7903078?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy","html_url":"https://github.com/tylerjereddy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":15,"created_at":"2024-11-08T03:33:03Z","updated_at":"2024-11-10T14:00:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Downstream in SciPy (see: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21830), when using GNU toolchain version `14.2.0` from homebrew, I consistently see a SciPy build failure on MacOS ARM because of `scipy/linalg/_flapackmodule.c:90:10: fatal error: threads.h: No such file or directory`.

I made a temporary hack (below) to my local NumPy/`f2py` source that fixed the SciPy build issue. I'm not sure if that's exactly what we'd want to do, though [GNU docs on the matter](https://www.gnu.org/software/gnulib/manual/html_node/threads_002eh.html#:~:text=This%20header%20file%20is%20missing,%2C%20MSVC%2014%2C%20Android%209.0.) do suggest that `threads.h` is missing on MacOS 14. This part of `f2py` has caused pain a few times in the past per gh-19437 and gh-24761.

```diff
--- a/numpy/f2py/cfuncs.py
+++ b/numpy/f2py/cfuncs.py
@@ -558,7 +558,8 @@ def errmess(s: str) -> None:
       && (__STDC_VERSION__ >= 201112L) \\
       && !defined(__STDC_NO_THREADS__) \\

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":17,"created_at":"2024-11-08T03:33:03Z","updated_at":"2024-11-10T14:46:05Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Downstream in SciPy (see: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21830), when using GNU toolchain version `14.2.0` from homebrew, I consistently see a SciPy build failure on MacOS ARM because of `scipy/linalg/_flapackmodule.c:90:10: fatal error: threads.h: No such file or directory`.

I made a temporary hack (below) to my local NumPy/`f2py` source that fixed the SciPy build issue. I'm not sure if that's exactly what we'd want to do, though [GNU docs on the matter](https://www.gnu.org/software/gnulib/manual/html_node/threads_002eh.html#:~:text=This%20header%20file%20is%20missing,%2C%20MSVC%2014%2C%20Android%209.0.) do suggest that `threads.h` is missing on MacOS 14. This part of `f2py` has caused pain a few times in the past per gh-19437 and gh-24761.

```diff
--- a/numpy/f2py/cfuncs.py
+++ b/numpy/f2py/cfuncs.py
@@ -558,7 +558,8 @@ def errmess(s: str) -> None:
       && (__STDC_VERSION__ >= 201112L) \\
       && !defined(__STDC_NO_THREADS__) \\
       && (!defined(__GLIBC__) || __GLIBC__ > 2 || (__GLIBC__ == 2 && __GLIBC_MINOR__ > 12)) \\
-      && !defined(NPY_OS_OPENBSD) && !defined(NPY_OS_HAIKU)
+      && !defined(NPY_OS_OPENBSD) && !defined(NPY_OS_HAIKU) \\
+      && !(defined(__APPLE__) && (__MAC_OS_X_VERSION_MAX_ALLOWED >= 140000 && __MAC_OS_X_VERSION_MAX_ALLOWED < 150000))
 /* __STDC_NO_THREADS__ was first defined in a maintenance release of glibc 2.12,
    see https://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/commit-hurd/2012-07/msg00180.html,
    so `!defined(__STDC_NO_THREADS__)` may give false positive for the existence
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2466763249","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27718#issuecomment-2466763249","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718","id":2466763249,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86TB9Hx","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-10T14:46:04Z","updated_at":"2024-11-10T14:46:04Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> you can't even build a fully functioning Python

I do not know how is “fully” defined, but I have certainly built Python with gcc on macOS, and it was functioning.

> Anything related to cryptography on macOS will need to use them

You mean their cryptography? That is unsurprising, since it uses ObjC (used at least, and perhaps nothing much changed). But there are plenty of open-source cryptography solutions not relying on Apple ObjC extensions.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2466763249/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

> 
> how about installing packages from PyPI ? (That is, `pip` etc. should be built with "real" gcc too).

I use MacPorts, I do not think anything fails with Python as such or C/C++ code in Python ports. Rust needs LLVM, so that won’t work with gcc, and there a few select packages which heavily depend on Apple SDK (and perhaps Apple ObjC syntax).

Referring to https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21826#issuecomment-2466445083

It is a half of what I had to patch, but these looks rather straightforward, since the same files currently mix C99 style with C++ style randomly in C++ sources, apparently because in most cases compilers do not complain much and different committers chose different code style. 

I.e.,
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/c45b3ba3df5ea9ebb517e98d62d138d0f9164724/scipy/special/boost_special_functions.h#L765
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/c45b3ba3df5ea9ebb517e98d62d138d0f9164724/scipy/special/boost_special_functions.h#L813
But:
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/c45b3ba3df5ea9ebb517e98d62d138d0f9164724/scipy/special/boost_special_functions.h#L76
Etc.

I am not including the rest, but if those look reasonable, I can add them too. See: https://github.com/barracuda156/scipy/commit/c8ea21327dbb48103bfa8d6349c6ee587f3b4a43
(Please ignore the old version of the patch which is currently in MacPorts, it was done for the old version of `scipy` and not in the best way Could you please review this? Referring to https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21826#issuecomment-2466445083

It is a half of what I had to patch, but these looks rather straightforward, since the same files currently mix C99 style with C++ style randomly in C++ sources, apparently because in most cases compilers do not complain much and different committers chose different code style. 

I.e.,
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/c45b3ba3df5ea9ebb517e98d62d138d0f9164724/scipy/special/boost_special_functions.h#L765
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/c45b3ba3df5ea9ebb517e98d62d138d0f9164724/scipy/special/boost_special_functions.h#L813
But:
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/c45b3ba3df5ea9ebb517e98d62d138d0f9164724/scipy/special/boost_special_functions.h#L76
Etc.

I am not including the rest, but if those look reasonable, I can add them too. See: https://github.com/barracuda156/scipy/commit/c8ea21327dbb48103bfa8d6349c6ee587f3b4a43
(Please ignore the old version of the patch which is currently in MacPorts, it was done for the old version of `scipy` and not in the best way Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":26,"created_at":"2024-11-08T03:33:03Z","updated_at":"2024-11-12T09:02:23Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Downstream in SciPy (see: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21830), when using GNU toolchain version `14.2.0` from homebrew, I consistently see a SciPy build failure on MacOS ARM because of `scipy/linalg/_flapackmodule.c:90:10: fatal error: threads.h: No such file or directory`.

I made a temporary hack (below) to my local NumPy/`f2py` source that fixed the SciPy build issue. I'm not sure if that's exactly what we'd want to do, though [GNU docs on the matter](https://www.gnu.org/software/gnulib/manual/html_node/threads_002eh.html#:~:text=This%20header%20file%20is%20missing,%2C%20MSVC%2014%2C%20Android%209.0.) do suggest that `threads.h` is missing on MacOS 14. This part of `f2py` has caused pain a few times in the past per gh-19437 and gh-24761.

```diff
--- a/numpy/f2py/cfuncs.py
+++ b/numpy/f2py/cfuncs.py
@@ -558,7 +558,8 @@ def errmess(s: str) -> None:
       && (__STDC_VERSION__ >= 201112L) \\
       && !defined(__STDC_NO_THREADS__) \\
       && (!defined(__GLIBC__) || __GLIBC__ > 2 || (__GLIBC__ == 2 && __GLIBC_MINOR__ > 12)) \\
-      && !defined(NPY_OS_OPENBSD) && !defined(NPY_OS_HAIKU)
+      && !defined(NPY_OS_OPENBSD) && !defined(NPY_OS_HAIKU) \\
+      && !(defined(__APPLE__) && (__MAC_OS_X_VERSION_MAX_ALLOWED >= 140000 && __MAC_OS_X_VERSION_MAX_ALLOWED < 150000))
 /* __STDC_NO_THREADS__ was first defined in a maintenance release of glibc 2.12,
    see https://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/commit-hurd/2012-07/msg00180.html,
    so `!defined(__STDC_NO_THREADS__)` may give false positive for the existence
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2469958202","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27718#issuecomment-2469958202","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718","id":2469958202,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86TOJI6","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-12T09:02:21Z","updated_at":"2024-11-12T09:02:21Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"@rgommers For some reason I do not get an error with `numpy` without patching this, maybe MacPorts uses the older `numpy` which does not have the issue? It is at 1.26.4 now.

For `scipy` the fix is needed though.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2469958202/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27718","id":2642730510,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86dhN4O","number":27718,"title":"BUG: f2py threads.h on MacOS 14.x may need a shim?","user":{"login":"tylerjereddy","id":7903078,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc5MDMwNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7903078?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy","html_url":"https://github.com/tylerjereddy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tylerjereddy/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":33,"created_at":"2024-11-08T03:33:03Z","updated_at":"2024-11-12T18:42:38Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Downstream in SciPy (see: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21830), when using GNU toolchain version `14.2.0` from homebrew, I consistently see a SciPy build failure on MacOS ARM because of `scipy/linalg/_flapackmodule.c:90:10: fatal error: threads.h: No such file or directory`.

I made a temporary hack (below) to my local NumPy/`f2py` source that fixed the SciPy build issue. I'm not sure if that's exactly what we'd want to do, though [GNU docs on the matter](https://www.gnu.org/software/gnulib/manual/html_node/threads_002eh.html#:~:text=This%20header%20file%20is%20missing,%2C%20MSVC%2014%2C%20Android%209.0.) do suggest that `threads.h` is missing on MacOS 14. This part of `f2py` has caused pain a few times in the past per gh-19437 and gh-24761.

```diff
--- a/numpy/f2py/cfuncs.py
+++ b/numpy/f2py/cfuncs.py
@@ -558,7 +558,8 @@ def errmess(s: str) -> None:
       && (__STDC_VERSION__ >= 201112L) \\
       && !defined(__STDC_NO_THREADS__) \\
       && (!defined(__GLIBC__) || __GLIBC__ > 2 || (__GLIBC__ == 2 && __GLIBC_MINOR__ > 12)) \\
-      && !defined(NPY_OS_OPENBSD) && !defined(NPY_OS_HAIKU)
+      && !defined(NPY_OS_OPENBSD) && !defined(NPY_OS_HAIKU) \\
+      && !(defined(__APPLE__) && (__MAC_OS_X_VERSION_MAX_ALLOWED >= 140000 && __MAC_OS_X_VERSION_MAX_ALLOWED < 150000))
 /* __STDC_NO_THREADS__ was first defined in a maintenance release of glibc 2.12,
    see https://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/commit-hurd/2012-07/msg00180.html,
    so `!defined(__STDC_NO_THREADS__)` may give false positive for the existence
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2471298725","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27718#issuecomment-2471298725","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27718","id":2471298725,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86TTQal","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-12T18:42:36Z","updated_at":"2024-11-12T18:42:36Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Running the build now, will update in a while.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2471298725/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846/events","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846","id":2389903681,"node_id":"I_kwDODZiDws6OcwlB","number":846,"title":"Should installation set include paths specifying GCC (gfortran) version?","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-07-04T04:20:25Z","updated_at":"2024-07-04T04:20:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I just noticed that installation sets paths in a form of `${chosen_prefix}/fortran_stdlib/GNU-13.3.0/` (as an example with the build using gcc13 13.3.0). Is this a) expected behavior and b) actually needed?

The issue is that we have multiple versions of GCC in MacPorts, but `fortran-stdlib` port just sets `+gfortran` variant, which is equivalent to using whichever GCC is used as a primary runtime supplier. So we end up with different pre-built tarballs having identical variants, which is obviously wrong.

If it does matter which exactly `gfortran` version is used, then we (in MacPorts) should change default variant to a specific GCC, plus we will have to revbump the port for every GCC minor version update, since that will also change.
If it does not matter which `gfortran` is used, then paths should be changed in a way to have them compatible across GCC versions, dropping `GNU-${version}` part altogether (whether locally in MacPorts or here in upstream).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Is this a) expected behavior and b) actually needed?

The issue is that we have multiple versions of GCC in MacPorts, but `fortran-stdlib` port just sets `+gfortran` variant, which is equivalent to using whichever GCC is used as a primary runtime supplier. So we end up with different pre-built tarballs having identical variants, which is obviously wrong.

If it does matter which exactly `gfortran` version is used, then we (in MacPorts) should change default variant to a specific GCC, plus we will have to revbump the port for every GCC minor version update, since that will also change.
If it does not matter which `gfortran` is used, then paths should be changed in a way to have them compatible across GCC versions, dropping `GNU-${version}` part altogether (whether locally in MacPorts or here in upstream).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/comments/2211659401","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846#issuecomment-2211659401","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846","id":2211659401,"node_id":"IC_kwDODZiDws6D0z6J","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-06T05:44:50Z","updated_at":"2024-07-06T05:44:50Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"@perazz Could you please comment on this one? Is this a) expected behavior and b) actually needed?

The issue is that we have multiple versions of GCC in MacPorts, but `fortran-stdlib` port just sets `+gfortran` variant, which is equivalent to using whichever GCC is used as a primary runtime supplier. So we end up with different pre-built tarballs having identical variants, which is obviously wrong.

If it does matter which exactly `gfortran` version is used, then we (in MacPorts) should change default variant to a specific GCC, plus we will have to revbump the port for every GCC minor version update, since that will also change.
If it does not matter which `gfortran` is used, then paths should be changed in a way to have them compatible across GCC versions, dropping `GNU-${version}` part altogether (whether locally in MacPorts or here in upstream).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/comments/2211696455","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846#issuecomment-2211696455","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/846","id":2211696455,"node_id":"IC_kwDODZiDws6D089H","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-06T07:47:10Z","updated_at":"2024-07-06T07:47:10Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"MacPorts What CMakeLists call Altivec is in fact VSX, which is a later ISA. It is misleading to use Altivec name.

This is on a system where Altivec is supported (but VSX is not):
```
/opt/local/var/macports/build/_opt_PPCSnowLeopardPorts_archivers_blosc2/blosc2/work/c-blosc2-2.13.2/blosc/shuffle-altivec.c:41:9: error: '__builtin_vsx_stxvw4x_v16qi' requires the '-mvsx' option
   41 |         vec_xst(xmm0[i], j + i * total_elements, dest);
      |         ^~~~~~~
/opt/local/var/macports/build/_opt_PPCSnowLeopardPorts_archivers_blosc2/blosc2/work/c-blosc2-2.13.2/blosc/shuffle-altivec.c:41:9: note: overloaded builtin '__builtin_vec_vsx_st' is implemented by builtin '__builtin_vsx_stxvw4x_v16qi'
/opt/local/var/macports/build/_opt_PPCSnowLeopardPorts_archivers_blosc2/blosc2/work/c-blosc2-2.13.2/blosc/shuffle-altivec.c: In function 'shuffle4_altivec':
/opt/local/var/macports/build/_opt_PPCSnowLeopardPorts_archivers_blosc2/blosc2/work/c-blosc2-2.13.2/blosc/shuffle-altivec.c:57:7: error: '__builtin_vsx_lxvw4x_v16qi' requires the '-mvsx' option
   57 |       xmm0[i] = vec_xl(bytesoftype * j + 16 * i, src);
      |       ^~~~
/opt/local/var/macports/build/_opt_PPCSnowLeopardPorts_archivers_blosc2/blosc2/work/c-blosc2-2.13.2/blosc/shuffle-altivec.c:57:7: note: overloaded builtin '__builtin_vec_vsx_ld' is implemented by builtin '__builtin_vsx_lxvw4x_v16qi'
/opt/local/var/macports/build/_opt_PPCSnowLeopardPorts_archivers_blosc2/blosc2/work/c-blosc2-2.13.2/blosc/shuffle-altivec.c:65:9: error: '__builtin_vsx_stxvw4x_v16qi' requires the '-mvsx' option
   65 |         vec_xst(xmm0[i], j + i*total_elements, dest);
      |         ^~~~~~~
```
The code explicitly uses VSX built-ins.

2. Is there some reason why VSX are allowed only for little-endian version (ppc64le)? Big-endian ppc64 supports VSX insns, though starting from ISA 2.06. (So it should not be enabled without a check, of course.)
If it is undesirable to have a check for supported ISA, then it can be done via non-default configure option. So that those who use VSX-capable POWER hardware in big-endian mode could benefit from hardware capabilities.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/c-blosc2/issues/592/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/c-blosc2/issues/592/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/c-blosc2/issues/comments/2155469570","html_url":"https://github.com/Blosc/c-blosc2/issues/592#issuecomment-2155469570","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/c-blosc2/issues/592","id":2155469570,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAmfImc6AedsC","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-07T20:00:58Z","updated_at":"2024-06-07T20:00:58Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"@kif 2. Is there some reason why VSX are allowed only for little-endian version (ppc64le)? Big-endian ppc64 supports VSX insns, though starting from ISA 2.06. (So it should not be enabled without a check, of course.)
If it is undesirable to have a check for supported ISA, then it can be done via non-default configure option. If it is technically impossible to link to TBB correctly, at least there should be a configure argument to do that from the user side.
I readily believe that it works correctly on a specific setup, but why not make it work robustly, on any coherent setup?

Re `cmdstanr`, though this is directly unrelated to the ticket: there are numerous reasons why a build which is not configurable may either fail or simply install in way which is not desired.

I will be happy to have `cmdstan` in MacPorts, whether via R or standalone, but I need installation instructions (assuming a build from source). If it is there, that is doable. But installing a complex OCaml software from within R sounds complicated :) 
If `cmdstanr` can be configured to use pre-installed Stanc3, OneTBB and whatever else it wants, then it easy, and I can make a port for it by tomorrow. Can it?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/stan-dev/rstan/issues/comments/2166285278/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

We were using bundled TBB, but we can certainly switch to using our standalone one (and that was already done for quanteda, for example, since it dropped RcppParallel support).
> 
> Also note that `rstan` isn't responsible for TBB linking, it simply [uses the flags provided by `RcppParallel`](https://github.com/stan-dev/rstan/blob/50c3c6f4e44a849b17af7e9e2b8656cd93238a18/rstan/rstan/R/plugin.R#L123).

@andrjohns You have pinpointed the exact cause of the issue:
```
macmini:~ svacchanda$ otool -L /opt/local/Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.3/Resources/library/RcppParallel/lib/libtbb.dylib
/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.3/Resources/library/RcppParallel/lib/libtbb.dylib:
	@rpath/libtbb.dylib (compatibility version 0.0.0, current version 0.0.0)
	/opt/local/lib/libgcc/libstdc++.6.dylib (compatibility version 7.0.0, current version 7.30.0)
	/opt/local/lib/libgcc/libgcc_s.1.1.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1.1.0)
	/usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 125.2.11)
```

For some reason this still works in most cases, including building `rstan` itself, but fails here. We can just use `install_name_tool` to fix the install path of `libtbb.dylib`, and that shouls solve the problem.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/stan-dev/rstan/issues/comments/2166508835/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Using rpaths is not wrong in itself, it is just not guaranteed to work. I am not really sure why `rstan` (or any dependent of `RcppParallel`) do not fail to load during build (`R` verifies that a package loads, if not, build gonna fail), and apparently most of those packages can be used (at least I certainly ran tests for quite a number of them, and if nothing would even load, it could not have gone unnoticed). Maybe the issue is in how `RcppParallel` communicates its flags?

In principle I would expect that _provided_ TBB is somehow found during the build of `rstan` and build completes successfully, the same should work for models which `rstan` compiles. However it is really my mistake that I did not notice that rpath in `RcppParallel`.

From my side I would like to see transparent [non-default] options to configure everything, even if the default build does not require any manual setup and works in vanilla CRAN environment. That makes it easy to fix possible issues without burdening upstream and without local hacks (which then require ongoing rebasing of patches).

> If there's some advice you have after doing this for RStan users, we can report it on the forums to try to make it indexable and also include in the docs.

Perhaps it makes sense to add a caution somewhere in documentation, that may save both users’ and your time. Maybe just make people aware of a possible failure – I do not have a fix for it (I could not find what exactly sets rpath in that package, and its build system is a nightmarish and buggy – not from R package side, but originally, from TBB itself).

I think it is pointless to try reporting `RcppParallel` issue to CRAN, since they tend to hold a view that if something works in their environment, that is sufficient. As long as that is an official position, any failures in alternative setups are not bugs: they are defined out :) 

P. S. By the way, for some reason `install_name_tool` trick failed for me with `RcppParallel` with some odd error; so I decided it is better to switch to using an external OneTBB instead. Many hours of rebuilding 80+ dependents of `RcppParallel` ahead LOL ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/stan-dev/rstan/issues/comments/2169242052/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The OS, compiler, and all programs involved should support `rpaths` without issue, it's only MacPorts that is having the problem

AFAIK, rpaths are meant for macOS bundles (where everything gets dumped in a single location, and an app is supposed to be movable) and not for Unix-style installations (user is not supposed to pull binaries randomly around). I am sure it is possible to force them to work in any environment, but why use a setup which is known to be fragile when absolute paths work perfectly well and do not require any trickery?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/stan-dev/rstan/issues/comments/2169281311/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/stan-dev/rstan/issues/979/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/stan-dev/rstan/issues/979/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/stan-dev/rstan/issues/comments/2169345550","html_url":"https://github.com/stan-dev/rstan/issues/979#issuecomment-2169345550","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/stan-dev/rstan/issues/979","id":2169345550,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAJwV286BTZYO","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-15T11:19:06Z","updated_at":"2024-06-15T11:19:06Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> But it's less about whether or not rpaths themselves are fragile - the only difference in this case between the linking working or not is whether MacPorts was used.

To establish this, we will need at least to reproduce the same installation without MacPorts involvement, that is, build everything manually with the same directory structure. It is a bit involving.

> If everything works without issue using Xcode or Homebrew compilers

(Xcode compilers do not support OpenMP, so they won’t work neatly with R.)

It is not hugely surprising though if Homebrew works, since numerous CRAN packages explicitly rely on Homebrew, configure scripts are using brewisms etc. This is not a bug in MacPorts if CRAN decides to pick a single package manager and ignore all alternatives. Admittedly, this is not rpath-specific, but if you try to run default builds of such packages outside of Homebrew environment, things break down badly.
One the other hand, if one uses our ports in our environment, everything also gonna “just work” (at least for R, on systems which we test).

> the TBB and RcppParallel use the exact same flags on linux/unix

I do not think so: https://github.com/RcppCore/RcppParallel/blob/3756f5b5b6d6948ae6e78771a314faef5e62476c/src/Makevars.in#L115-L117
And this is perhaps working fine on Linux, but it does not work fine with Apple linker: https://github.com/RcppCore/RcppParallel/blob/3756f5b5b6d6948ae6e78771a314faef5e62476c/src/Makevars.in#L15-L17 (so it should not use the same flags in fact). Mac version will be `PKG_LIBS = -Wl,-L"$(TBB_LIB)" -Wl,-rpath,"$(TBB_LIB)" -ltbb -ltbbmalloc` – otherwise linking may fail.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/stan-dev/rstan/issues/comments/2169345550/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

We provide packages in Macports for macOS users. CRAN has a rather abysmal macOS support :) 

> Can you create a PR that passes on 32-bit ppc but is innocuous for other platforms?

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/c-blosc2/issues/592","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/c-blosc2","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/c-blosc2/issues/592/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/c-blosc2/issues/592/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/Blosc/c-blosc2/issues/592/events","html_url":"https://github.com/Blosc/c-blosc2/issues/592","id":2193562252,"node_id":"I_kwDOAmfImc6CvxqM","number":592,"title":"Altivec option is misleading, it should be VSX; also, why only ppc64le, but not ppc64 (for supported CPUs)?","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-03-18T22:39:57Z","updated_at":"2024-03-18T22:39:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"1. What CMakeLists call Altivec is in fact VSX, which is a later ISA. It is misleading to use Altivec name.

This is on a system where Altivec is supported (but VSX is not):
```
/opt/local/var/macports/build/_opt_PPCSnowLeopardPorts_archivers_blosc2/blosc2/work/c-blosc2-2.13.2/blosc/shuffle-altivec.c:41:9: error: '__builtin_vsx_stxvw4x_v16qi' requires the '-mvsx' option
   41 |         vec_xst(xmm0[i], j + i * total_elements, dest);
      |         ^~~~~~~
/opt/local/var/macports/build/_opt_PPCSnowLeopardPorts_archivers_blosc2/blosc2/work/c-blosc2-2.13.2/blosc/shuffle-altivec.c:41:9: note: overloaded builtin '__builtin_vec_vsx_st' is implemented by builtin '__builtin_vsx_stxvw4x_v16qi'
/opt/local/var/macports/build/_opt_PPCSnowLeopardPorts_archivers_blosc2/blosc2/work/c-blosc2-2.13.2/blosc/shuffle-altivec.c: In function 'shuffle4_altivec':

Exit code: 1
```

### Expected Behaviour

Hopefully, it can be made to build, since it not only builds but also works on 32-bit PowerPC (tested on the same 10.6 build 10A190).

0.10.0

### Platform and Architecture

macOS 10.6 / i386

Macports build env.

working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-02-05T23:17:35Z","updated_at":"2024-02-12T21:36:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Description

Trying to build FPM on macOS i386 resulted in linking error:
```
Undefined symbols for architecture i386:
  "_opendir$INODE64", referenced from:
      _c_opendir in libfpm.a(src_filesystem_utilities.c.o)
ld: symbol(s) not found for architecture i386
collect2: error: ld returned 1 exit status
<ERROR> Compilation failed for object " fpm "
<ERROR> stopping due to failed compilation
STOP 1
Command failed:  cd "/opt/local/var/macports/build/_opt_local_var_macports_sources_rsync.macports.org_macports_release_tarballs_ports_devel_fpm/fpm/work/fpm-0.10.0" && FPM_CFLAGS="-Os -arch i386" FPM_LDFLAGS="-L/opt/local/lib -Wl,-headerpad_max_install_names -arch i386" /opt/local/var/macports/build/_opt_local_var_macports_sources_rsync.macports.org_macports_release_tarballs_ports_devel_fpm/fpm/work/fpm-0.10.0/install.sh 
Exit code: 1
```

### Expected Behaviour

Hopefully, it can be made to build, since it not only builds but also works on 32-bit PowerPC (tested on the same 10.6 build 10A190).

### Version of fpm

0.10.0

### Platform and Architecture

macOS 10.6 / i386

### Additional Information

Macports build env.
See also: https://trac.macports.org/ticket/69278","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/993/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/993/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/comments/1939625499","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/993#issuecomment-1939625499","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/993","id":1939625499,"node_id":"IC_kwDODe7zws5znFYb","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-02-12T21:36:14Z","updated_at":"2024-02-12T21:36:14Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Here is what `libSystem` has:
```
$ nm -arch i386 /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib | grep opendir
00081276 T ___opendir2
0006942b T ___opendir2$INODE64$UNIX2003
0002804a T ___opendir2$UNIX2003
0007ec14 T _opendir
00069410 T _opendir$INODE64$UNIX2003
0002802f T _opendir$UNIX2003
```
Here is what `fpm` sets:
```
#if defined(__APPLE__) && !defined(__aarch64__) && !defined(__POWERPC__)
DIR * opendir$INODE64( const char * dirName );
struct dirent * readdir$INODE64( DIR * dir );
#define opendir opendir$INODE64
#define readdir readdir$INODE64
#endif
```
This PR added exclusion for PowerPC: https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm/pull/824
This PR for arm64: https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/547

In fact I am not completely sure we fixed it earlier in a correct way even for PowerPC. Notice:
```
$ nm -arch ppc /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib | grep opendir
000a0118 T ___opendir2
0008119c T ___opendir2$INODE64$UNIX2003
0000e1c8 T ___opendir2$UNIX2003
0009ca4c T _opendir
00081194 T _opendir$INODE64$UNIX2003
0000e1c0 T _opendir$UNIX2003
```
Even though `fpm` appears to work fine on `ppc` with existing code. (At least, no related errors were seen, and I do build a number of ports using `fpm` for `ppc`.)

There are two questions:
1. For 32-bit archs (`ppc` and `i386`), should we actually skip the block or use it in a modified way?
2. For `ppc64`, likely, INODE64 should be used, though I cannot check symbols in the libSystem right now (to be updated). If yes, then condition should refer to `__ppc__`, not `__POWERPC__`.

@awvwgk What do you think?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/comments/1939625499/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Hopefully, it can be made to build, since it not only builds but also works on 32-bit PowerPC (tested on the same 10.6 build 10A190).

investigating #390, I came across an interesting finding: L-BFGS was computing negative scaling values for the Hessian scaling step.  (These should not be negative!)  Specifically, the gradient norm was being computed as *negative* and this then snowballed into a convergence issue.  As it turns out, the convergence issue only seems to manifest on non-x86_64 architectures when using `arma::fmat` likely due to numerical precision specifics and other things along those lines.  Here's an example run of the `Johnson844LovaszThetaFMatSDP` test, printing negative gradient norms:

```
$ ./ensmallen_tests Johnson844LovaszThetaFMatSDP
ensmallen version: 2.21.0 (Bent Antenna)
armadillo version: 9.800.1 (Horizon Scraper)
random seed: 0
Filters: Johnson844LovaszThetaFMatSDP
Scaling factor less than zero! -0.0215062
Scaling factor less than zero! -2.41463e-09
===============================================================================
All tests passed (1122 assertions in 1 test case)
```

So, first question is: @conradsnicta do you consider it a responsibility of Armadillo to provide a nonnegative norm?  If so, I can add an extra `if (norm < 0) return 0` type check to the `norm()` function and open an MR.  If not, I'll just keep that in mind and check throughout ensmallen to make sure we don't depend on `norm()` returning nonnegative values.

Inside of ensmallen I chose to fix the issue by avoiding division by the gradient norm (or other quantities) when they are very small or negative.  On the M1 Macbook I have sitting around, this fixes the test failure and convergence succeeds.

I also fixed some compilation warnings, and made `AugLagrangian` not output the entire set of coordinates when `ENS_PRINT_INFO` is enabled (the coordinates could be huge!).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/mlpack/ensmallen/issues/392/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/mlpack/ensmallen/issues/392/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/mlpack/ensmallen/issues/comments/1963322357","html_url":"https://github.com/mlpack/ensmallen/pull/392#issuecomment-1963322357","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/mlpack/ensmallen/issues/392","id":1963322357,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCQVjp851Bev1","user":{"login":"barracuda156","id":92015510,"node_id":"U_kgDOBXwLlg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/92015510?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156","html_url":"https://github.com/barracuda156","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barracuda156/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-02-26T05:01:03Z","updated_at":"2024-02-26T05:01:03Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"@conradsnicta It should be possible to compare cases of using OpenBLAS vs Accelerate (and maybe some alternative BLAS implementations too).

Pandas version checks

- [X] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
While trying to build this on NetBSD, the following appears in the build log:


pandas/_libs/window/aggregations.cpp: In function 'void __pyx_f_6pandas_5_libs_6window_12aggregations_add_mean(__pyx_t_5numpy_float64_t, Py_ssize_t*, __pyx_t_5numpy_float64_t*, Py_ssize_t*, __pyx_t_5numpy_float64_t*, __pyx_t_5numpy_int64_t*, __pyx_t_5numpy_float64_t*)':
pandas/_libs/window/aggregations.cpp:4784:18: error: 'signbit' was not declared in this scope; did you mean 'std::signbit'?
 4784 |     __pyx_t_1 = (signbit(__pyx_v_val) != 0);
      |                  ^~~~~~~
      |                  std::signbit
```

This happens both on NetBSD/amd64 and NetBSD/powerpc, with pandas version 1.5.3.


### Issue Description

See above for the build error.

The reason for this build failure on NetBSD is that even though `<math.h>` is included, 
the `<complex>` C++ header is included later, and that includes `<cmath>` which `#undef`s 
the macros defined in `<math.h>`, among them `signbit()`.  So at this point, you have to use 
the `std::signbit` variant instead, and this has consequences for how those functions should 
be imported into the `aggregations.pyx` file.  This used to be OK in pandas version 1.3.5, but 
things have changed in 1.5.3 so that this no longer builds on NetBSD.

I have a suggested change lined up which re-does the import of `signbit()` and `sqrt()` to come 
from the `std::` namespace, but don't know if there is a particular reason the change between 
1.3.5 and 1.5.3 was introduced.

### Expected Behavior

The build should succeed.

Pandas version checks

- [X] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
While trying to build this on NetBSD, the following appears in the build log:


pandas/_libs/window/aggregations.cpp: In function 'void __pyx_f_6pandas_5_libs_6window_12aggregations_add_mean(__pyx_t_5numpy_float64_t, Py_ssize_t*, __pyx_t_5numpy_float64_t*, Py_ssize_t*, __pyx_t_5numpy_float64_t*, __pyx_t_5numpy_int64_t*, __pyx_t_5numpy_float64_t*)':
pandas/_libs/window/aggregations.cpp:4784:18: error: 'signbit' was not declared in this scope; did you mean 'std::signbit'?
 4784 |     __pyx_t_1 = (signbit(__pyx_v_val) != 0);
      |                  ^~~~~~~
      |                  std::signbit
```

This happens both on NetBSD/amd64 and NetBSD/powerpc, with pandas version 1.5.3.


### Issue Description

See above for the build error.

The reason for this build failure on NetBSD is that even though `<math.h>` is included, 
the `<complex>` C++ header is included later, and that includes `<cmath>` which `#undef`s 
the macros defined in `<math.h>`, among them `signbit()`.  So at this point, you have to use 
the `std::signbit` variant instead, and this has consequences for how those functions should 
be imported into the `aggregations.pyx` file.  This used to be OK in pandas version 1.3.5, but 
things have changed in 1.5.3 so that this no longer builds on NetBSD.

I have a suggested change lined up which re-does the import of `signbit()` and `sqrt()` to come 
from the `std::` namespace, but don't know if there is a particular reason the change between 
1.3.5 and 1.5.3 was introduced.

### Expected Behavior

The build should succeed.

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-12-17T11:01:47Z","updated_at":"2023-12-17T11:01:47Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When trying to build `numpy`, I get the following error during evaluating BLAS variants:
```
../../numpy/meson.build:119:13: ERROR: Unhandled python exception

    This is a Meson bug and should be reported!

ERROR Backend subprocess exited when trying to invoke build_wheel
```
The issue appears specific to `pep517` backend: on the same system, it does not happen if `pep517` is disabled.

See also:
https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson-python/issues/554
https://trac.macports.org/ticket/68908#comment:10

@rgommers Confirmed the same failure on 10.6.8 `x86_64` (so error is unrelated to PowerPC or Rosetta).

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
This is a build (or rather configure) error. What seems to fail is this chunk:

# First try scipy-openblas, and if found don't look for cblas or lapack, we
# know what's inside the scipy-openblas wheels already.
if blas_name == 'openblas' or blas_name == 'auto'
  blas = dependency('scipy-openblas', required: false)
  if blas.found()
    blas_name = 'scipy-openblas'
  endif
endif
if blas_name == 'auto'
  foreach _name : get_option('blas-order')
    if _name == 'mkl'
      blas = dependency('mkl',
        modules: ['cblas'] + blas_interface + mkl_opts,
        required: false,  # may be required, but we need to emit a custom error message
        version: mkl_version_req,
      )
      # Insert a second try with MKL, because we may be rejecting older versions
      # or missing it because no pkg-config installed. If so, we need to retry
      # with MKL SDL, and drop the version constraint (this always worked).
      if not blas.found() and mkl_may_use_sdl
        blas = dependency('mkl', modules: ['cblas', 'sdl: true'], required: false)
      endif
    else
      if _name == 'flexiblas' and use_ilp64
        _name = 'flexiblas64'
      endif
      blas = dependency(_name, modules: ['cblas'] + blas_interface, required: false)
    endif
    if blas.found()
      break
    endif
  endforeach
else
  if blas_name == 'mkl'
    blas = dependency('mkl',
      modules: ['cblas'] + blas_interface + mkl_opts,
      required: false,
      version: mkl_version_req,
    )
    # Same deal as above - try again for MKL
    if not blas.found() and mkl_may_use_sdl
      blas = dependency('mkl', modules: ['cblas', 'sdl: true'], required: false)
    endif
  else
    blas = dependency(blas_name, modules: ['cblas'] + blas_interface, required: false)
  endif
endif
```
```

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-12-17T11:01:47Z","updated_at":"2023-12-17T11:21:19Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When trying to build `numpy`, I get the following error during evaluating BLAS variants:
```
../../numpy/meson.build:119:13: ERROR: Unhandled python exception

    This is a Meson bug and should be reported!

ERROR Backend subprocess exited when trying to invoke build_wheel
```
The issue appears specific to `pep517` backend: on the same system, it does not happen if `pep517` is disabled.

See also:
https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson-python/issues/554
https://trac.macports.org/ticket/68908#comment:10

@rgommers Confirmed the same failure on 10.6.8 `x86_64` (so error is unrelated to PowerPC or Rosetta).

### Reproduce the code example:

This is a build (or rather configure) error. What seems to fail is this chunk:
```
# First try scipy-openblas, and if found don't look for cblas or lapack, we
# know what's inside the scipy-openblas wheels already.
if blas_name == 'openblas' or blas_name == 'auto'
  blas = dependency('scipy-openblas', required: false)
  if blas.found()
    blas_name = 'scipy-openblas'
  endif
endif
if blas_name == 'auto'
  foreach _name : get_option('blas-order')
    if _name == 'mkl'
      blas = dependency('mkl',
        modules: ['cblas'] + blas_interface + mkl_opts,
        required: false,  # may be required, but we need to emit a custom error message
        version: mkl_version_req,
      )
      # Insert a second try with MKL, because we may be rejecting older versions
      # or missing it because no pkg-config installed. If so, we need to retry
      # with MKL SDL, and drop the version constraint (this always worked).
      if not blas.found() and mkl_may_use_sdl
        blas = dependency('mkl', modules: ['cblas', 'sdl: true'], required: false)
      endif
    else
      if _name == 'flexiblas' and use_ilp64
        _name = 'flexiblas64'
      endif
      blas = dependency(_name, modules: ['cblas'] + blas_interface, required: false)
    endif
    if blas.found()
      break
    endif
  endforeach
else
  if blas_name == 'mkl'
    blas = dependency('mkl',
      modules: ['cblas'] + blas_interface + mkl_opts,
      required: false,
      version: mkl_version_req,
    )
    # Same deal as above - try again for MKL
    if not blas.found() and mkl_may_use_sdl
      blas = dependency('mkl', modules: ['cblas', 'sdl: true'], required: false)
    endif
  else
    blas = dependency(blas_name, modules: ['cblas'] + blas_interface, required: false)
  endif
endif
```

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-12-17T11:01:47Z","updated_at":"2023-12-18T13:16:13Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When trying to build `numpy`, I get the following error during evaluating BLAS variants:
```
../../numpy/meson.build:119:13: ERROR: Unhandled python exception

    This is a Meson bug and should be reported!

ERROR Backend subprocess exited when trying to invoke build_wheel
```
The issue appears specific to `pep517` backend: on the same system, it does not happen if `pep517` is disabled.

See also:
https://github.com/mesonbuild/meson-python/issues/554
https://trac.macports.org/ticket/68908#comment:10

@rgommers Confirmed the same failure on 10.6.8 `x86_64` (so error is unrelated to PowerPC or Rosetta).

### Reproduce the code example:

This is a build (or rather configure) error. What seems to fail is this chunk:
```
# First try scipy-openblas, and if found don't look for cblas or lapack, we
# know what's inside the scipy-openblas wheels already.
if blas_name == 'openblas' or blas_name == 'auto'
  blas = dependency('scipy-openblas', required: false)
  if blas.found()
    blas_name = 'scipy-openblas'
  endif
endif
if blas_name == 'auto'
  foreach _name : get_option('blas-order')
    if _name == 'mkl'
      blas = dependency('mkl',
        modules: ['cblas'] + blas_interface + mkl_opts,
        required: false,  # may be required, but we need to emit a custom error message
        version: mkl_version_req,
      )
      # Insert a second try with MKL, because we may be rejecting older versions
      # or missing it because no pkg-config installed. If so, we need to retry
      # with MKL SDL, and drop the version constraint (this always worked).
      if not blas.found() and mkl_may_use_sdl
        blas = dependency('mkl', modules: ['cblas', 'sdl: true'], required: false)
      endif
    else
      if _name == 'flexiblas' and use_ilp64
        _name = 'flexiblas64'
      endif
      blas = dependency(_name, modules: ['cblas'] + blas_interface, required: false)
    endif
    if blas.found()
      break
    endif
  endforeach
else
  if blas_name == 'mkl'
    blas = dependency('mkl',
      modules: ['cblas'] + blas_interface + mkl_opts,
      required: false,
      version: mkl_version_req,
    )
    # Same deal as above - try again for MKL
    if not blas.found() and mkl_may_use_sdl
      blas = dependency('mkl', modules: ['cblas', 'sdl: true'], required: false)
    endif
  else
    blas = dependency(blas_name, modules: ['cblas'] + blas_interface, required: false)
  endif
endif
```

Specifically, this code in `mesonbuild/dependencies/blas_lapack.py` breaks the build:

```
    def check_macOS_recent_enough(self) -> bool:
        # We need the SDK to be >=13.3 (meaning at least XCode 14.3)
        cmd = ['xcrun', '-sdk', 'macosx', '--show-sdk-version']
        sdk_version = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, check=True, text=True).stdout.strip()
        macos_version = platform.mac_ver()[0]
        deploy_target = os.environ.get('MACOSX_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET', macos_version)
        return sdk_version >= '13.3' and deploy_target >= '13.3'
```
There are two problems here:
1. The syntax used is unsupported on macOS < 10.8.
```
svacchanda$ xcrun -sdk macosx --show-sdk-version
xcrun: error: unrecognized option: --show-sdk-version

usage: 
xcrun [-verbose] [-no-cache] [-kill-cache] [-sdk <sdkroot>] [-log] [-run] <utility> [utility argument(s) ...]
xcrun [-verbose] [-no-cache] [-kill-cache] [-sdk <sdkroot>] -find <utility> 
<tool> [tool arguments ...]
```
2. Restriction to the OS version is totally unjustified: I can say for the fact that `numpy` 1.26.2 builds fine at least on 10.6 (and I am reasonably sure it will build on 10.5 too).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1860473769/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
